items.question: What is the Big-O of `T(n) = 3n² + 100n + 5000`?
items.explanation: Drop constants and lower-order terms; n² dominates.

items.question: Which is the correct ordering from slowest to fastest growth?
options: O(n²) < O(n log n) < O(n) < O(1)
O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²)
items.answer: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²)
items.explanation: Classic growth-rate ladder from slowest-growing to fastest-growing.

items.question: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array of n elements?
items.explanation: Each step halves the search space.

items.question: If doubling n approximately quadruples runtime, what is the algorithm's complexity?
Why is Python's `list.append` described as **amortized** O(1)?
options: Most appends are O(1) but occasional resizes are O(n); averaged, per-append cost is constant
Constants matter so it's effectively O(n)
items.answer: Most appends are O(1) but occasional resizes are O(n); averaged, per-append cost is constant
items.explanation: Amortized analysis spreads the O(n) resize across the many cheap appends.

items.question: Which expression is NOT equivalent to the others as a Big-O class?
items.explanation: The first three are all O(n); only O(n log n) is a different growth class.
